channel.title: 99% Invisible
channel.description: Design is everywhere in our lives, perhaps most importantly in the places where we've just stopped noticing. 99% Invisible is a weekly exploration of the process and power of design and architecture. From award winning producer Roman Mars. Learn more at 99percentinvisible.org.

item.title: 99% Noise
item.description: This episode of 99% Invisible is all about acoustic design, the city soundscape, and how to make listening in shared spaces pleasant (or at the very least, possible). It features an interview with Dennis Paoletti from Shen Milsom & Wilke.

In the beginning, former AIA-SF president Henrik Bull and the Transamerica Pyramid did not get along. The building was an affront to late 1960’s modernist ideals. It was silly. It looked like a dunce cap. Its large scale had no … Continue reading →

item.title: 99% Reality (only)
item.description: There’s not much that we can do about all the physical matter that’s been designed and built by someone else. It is the way it is. But with the advent of portable devices with GPS, a compass, and a network, … Continue reading →

item.title: 99% Details
item.description: It’s a stick with bristles poking out of it. It doesn’t even qualify as a simple machine, but the careful thought and design that went into the creation of the modern, angled bristle, fat handled toothbrush shows just how much … Continue reading →

item.title: 99% Forgotten
item.description: At the top of Mt. Olympus in San Francisco, on what was once thought to be the geographic center of the city, is a pedestal for a statue that isn’t there. There’s no marker. You can just make out the … Continue reading →

item.title: 99% Symbolic
item.description: Before I moved to Chicago in 2005, I didn’t even know cities had their own flags. In Chicago, the city flag is everywhere. It’s incorporated into all different aspects of city life and the design elements are used on businesses, … Continue reading →

item.title: 99% Alien
item.description: Humans need a few basic things to survive- air, water, food, heat, shelter- but just surviving isn’t really enough. We also need familiarity, a little comfort, interaction, a small place of our own. When it comes to designing space habitat … Continue r...

item.title: 99% Free Parking
item.description: It’s weird how much anxiety comes from parking in a city. Beyond the stress of looking for parking, you must contend with the frequently unreliable meters. The signage can be indecipherable. As a point of interaction with your municipality,

item.title: 99% Private
item.description: Privately Owned Public Open Spaces, or POPOS, are these little gardens, terraces, plazas, and seating areas that are private property, but are mandated for public use. City planners require developers to add these little “parks” to their buildings to m...

item.title: One Way Ticket To Mars
item.description: 99% Invisible Extra! NASA is figuring out how to take the next great leap into space. The difficulty is, if we leap to Mars, we might not make it back. This is a story I produced last year (Summer 2009) … Continue reading →

item.title: 99% Sound and Feel
item.description: Chris Downey explains it like this, “Beethoven continued to write music, even some of his best music, after he lost his hearing…What’s more preposterous, composing music you can’t hear, or designing architecture you can’t see?

item.title: 99% Undesigned
item.description: Almost everything in modern life is designed to waste energy. The whole system evolved on a false premise that petroleum is cheap and plentiful and will be that way forever. The awesome Lisa Margonelli, author of Oil on The Brain … Continue reading →

item.title: 99% Guilt Free
item.description: “Sustainable Design is a design philosophy that seeks to maximize the quality of the built environment, while minimizing or eliminating the negative impact to the natural environment.” -Jason F. McLennan, The Philosophy of Sustainable Design I like McL...

item.title: 13- Maps
item.description: I’m sorry, but if you don’t love maps, I don’t think we can be friends anymore. Maps are amazing. They are art and story. A representation of where we are and where we wish we could be. They’ve always had … Continue reading →

item.title: Game Over (Snap Judgment)
item.description: 99% Invisible Extra! The tape rolls as we witness the tearful end of a perfect online world. This is a piece I did for Snap Judgment, based on a story from Robert Ashley’s brilliant A Life Well Wasted internet radio … Continue reading →

item.title: Periodic Table
item.description: Everyone knows it when they see it. The classic “castle with turrets” periodic table is a beautiful and concise icon that contains a great deal of amazing information, if you only know how to read it. And even if you … Continue reading →

item.title: Sounds of the Artificial World
item.description: Without all the beeps and chimes, without sonic feedback, all of your modern conveniences would be very hard to use. If a device and its sounds are designed correctly, it creates a special “theater of the mind” that users completely … Continue reading →

item.title: A Designed Language
item.description: The idea is simple and quite beautiful: if we all shared a second, politically neutral language, people of all different nations and cultures could communicate freely and easily, and it would foster international understanding and peace.

item.title: Concrete Furniture
item.description: The New City Hall, designed by Finnish architect Viljo Revell, was the first modern, concrete, civic building in Toronto. When it opened in 1965, it stood out very prominently in the traditional Victorian fabric of the city.

item.title: Check Cashing Stores
item.description: A few years ago, journalist Douglas McGray learned that the largest chain of check cashing stores in Southern California, Nix Check Cashing, was being bought by the nation’s largest credit union, Kinecta. The credit union thought it had something to … ...

item.title: Liberation Squares plus NY Dick
item.description: In a recent piece from Urban Omnibus, Vishaan Chakrabarti (Professor at the Graduate School for Architecture, Planning and Preservation at Columbia University), wrote about how urban open spaces contribute to political change,

item.title: RJDJ Reactive Music
item.description: This week, the radio audience heard episode #10, but for you web and podcast listeners, I have a story I did about a year and a half ago, about the reactive music app called RJDJ. I did this piece for … Continue reading →

item.title: Nikko Concrete Commando
item.description: In 2001, Delfin Vigil was walking the streets of San Francisco and ran across the name “Nikko” carved into the concrete sidewalk. After seeing Nikko once, Delfin began to see the name everywhere. One block after another, there he was … Continue reading →

item.title: BLDGBLOG: On Sound
item.description: Most sound design in architecture is centered around designing for silence. Buildings are trying to block out that constant stream noise from the street and insulate you from those jarring clangs of industry.

item.title: Free Speech Monument
item.description: In 1989, a group called the Berkeley Art Project decided to hold a national public art competition to create a monument that would commemorate the 25th anniversary of the Free Speech Movement, which began on the University of California Berkeley … Cont...

item.title: You Are Listening To + Radio Net
item.description: youarelistening.to appeared online on March 6, 2011 and I was hooked instantly. The combination of the police scanner and ambient music is an intriguing, and distinctly live, experience (unlike most of the time shifted audio I tend to consume).

item.title: The Capitol Columns
item.description: If you were present for any of the presidential inaugurations, from Andrew Jackson to Dwight D. Eisenhower, you saw the solemn oath of office taken between twenty-two smooth, sandstone columns at the East Portico of the U.S. Capitol Building.

item.title: Unsung Icons of Soviet Design
item.description: There’s something that links most of the everyday objects presented in “Made in Russia: Unsung Icons of Soviet Design.” But it’s hard to tell exactly what that is just by looking at this collection of wobbly dolls, drinking glasses,

item.title: Chicago’s Jailhouse Skyscraper
item.description: The Metropolitan Correctional Center, or MCC, is a federal jail right in the middle of downtown Chicago. It’s a triangle-shaped skyscraper, 27 stories, with tall, super-narrow, irregularly-spaced windows up and down each wall.

item.title: Bridge to the Sky
item.description: There are rules that dicate what you can build and how. Rules of physics and rules of men who sit on various bureaucratic boards and bodies. These rules dictated that if silk magnate John Noble Stearns wanted to build one … Continue reading →

item.title: Movie Title Sequences
item.description: More and more I’m finding that the first 2-3 minutes of a movie are my favorite part of the film. My life is devoted to the beautiful expression of information, which is why film title sequences hold a special place … Continue reading →

item.title: Cul de Sac
item.description: When people critique cul-de-sacs, a lot of the time, they’re actually critiquing the suburbs more generally. The cul-de-sac has become sort of like the mascot of the suburbs– like if suburbia had a flag, it would have a picture of … Continue reading →

item.title: The Blue Yarn
item.description: In 1998 Dr. Gary Kaplan, the CEO of Virginia Mason Medical Center in Seattle received some bad news about his hospital. It was losing money. So Dr. Kaplan started studying how other hospitals were being run to see if there … Continue reading →

item.title: Feltron Annual Report
item.description: Nicholas Felton is an information designer. Since 2005, he has tabulated thousands upon thousands of tiny measurements in his life and designed stunning graphs and maps and created concise infographics that detail that year’s activities.

item.title: Design for Airports
item.description: When I spoke with Allison Arieff about the design of airports, she said to me, if all airports simply played Brian Eno’s album Ambient 1: Music for Airports over the speakers, every airport would be better. I say this to … Continue reading →

item.title: A Cheer for Samuel Plimsoll
item.description: If you look at the outer hull of commercial ships, you might find a painted circle bisected with a long horizontal line. This marking is called the load line, or as I prefer, the Plimsoll line. This simple graphic design … Continue reading →

item.title: The Speed of Light for Building Pyramids
item.description: Last year, Steve Burrows CBE (Principle at the engineering consulting firm Arup) spent several weeks in Egypt studying the pyramids through the eyes of a modern day structural engineer. The result, which was presented in a documentary for the Discovery...

item.title: Elegy for WTC
item.description: I want to be careful not to overstate what it means for a building to die. A building’s worth is an infinitesimal fraction of the worth a person’s life. Even two buildings don’t even move the needle in comparison to … Continue reading →

item.title: Super Bon Bonn
item.description: Cities are pretty robust organisms, they tend to survive even when put under tremendous stress and strain. Local industries rise and fall, people immigrate and emigrate, but most of these changes happen over decades.

item.title: The Steering Wheel
item.description: If I asked you to close your eyes and mimic the action of using one of the simple human interfaces of everyday life, you could probably do it. Without having a button to push, you could close your eyes and … Continue reading →

item.description: If Dennis Baxter and Bill Whiston are doing their job right, you probably don’t notice that they’re doing their job. But they are so good at doing their job, that you probably don’t even know that their job exists at … Continue reading →

item.title: Darth Vader Family Courthouse
item.description: It’s hard to imagine a place where more desperate and depressing drama unfolds on a daily basis than a family courthouse- custody battles, abuse, divorce- and if you were to design a place to reflect and amplify that misery, not … Continue reading →

item.title: The Biography of 100,000 Square Feet
item.description: United Nations Plaza sits in the center of San Francisco. Most people consider it a complete failure as a public space. Its central feature, at the entrance of the plaza, is a unique fountain that was designed by Lawrence Halprin … Continue reading →

item.title: Billy Possum
item.description: It’s totally unfair. Hydrox cookies came out four years before the introduction of Oreos, but Hydrox could never shake the image that it was a cheap knock-off, an also-ran. As a consumer product, it’s completely out of your hands if … Continue reading →

item.title: The Human-Human Interface
item.description: Paola Antonelli is the Senior Curator in the Department of Architecture and Design at the Museum of Modern Art. Her most recent blockbuster show, Talk to Me, explored the communication between people and objects: from chairs that talk to subway … Conti...

item.title: Accidental Music of Imperfect Escalators
item.description: “There’s a secret jazz seeping from Washington’s aging Metro escalators – those anemic metal walkways that fill our transit system…they honk and bleat and squawk…why are you still wearing those earbuds?” -Chris Richards,

item.title: The Pruitt-Igoe Myth
item.description: The Pruitt-Igoe housing project in St. Louis became most famous at the moment of its demise. The thirty-three high-rise towers built in the 1950’s were supposed to solve the impending population crisis in inner city St. Louis.

item.title: Immersive Ideal
item.description: Beauty Pill is band I really like from Washington DC. They have released two EPs (The Cigarette Girl From the Future and You Are Right to be Afraid) and their last album, The Unsustainable Lifestyle, came out in 2004. In … Continue reading →

item.title: Vulcanite Dentures
item.description: Before the 1850s, dentures were made out of very hard, very painful and very expensive material, like gold or ivory. They were a luxury item. The invention of Vulcanite hard rubber changed everything. It was moldable,

item.title: US Postal Service Stamps
item.description: Somebody might be able to do a great painting that’s 20 x 30 inches, but you take that down to 1 x 1.5 inches, and it’s a challenge to make it work. -Ethel Kessler, Art Director for USPS Stamp Services … Continue reading →

item.title: The Bathtubs or the Boiler Room
item.description: “I have this habit of walking into any door that’s unlocked…You start poking around, going into doors…you find the coolest things…” -Andrea Seabrook, NPR Congressional Correspondent In the eight years Andrea Seabrook has been reporting on Congress,

item.title: Queue Theory and Design
item.description: In the US, it’s called a line. In Canada, it’s often referred to as a line-up. Pretty much everywhere else, it’s known as a queue. My friend Benjamen Walker is obsessed with queues. He keeps sending me YouTube clips of … Continue reading →

item.title: DeafSpace
item.description: The acoustics of a building are a big concern for architects. But for designers at Gallaudet University in Washington, DC, it’s the absence of sound that defines the approach to architecture. Gallaudet is a university dedicated to educating the deaf … ...

item.title: The Arsenal of Exclusion
item.description: “Cities exist to bring people together, but cities can also keep people apart” – Daniel D’Oca, Urban Planner, Interboro Partners. Cities are great. They have movement, activity and diversity. But go to any city and it’s pretty clear,

item.title: Galloping Gertie
item.description: Even during the construction of the original Tacoma Narrows Bridge, the deck would go up and down by several feet with the slightest breeze. Construction workers on the span chewed on lemon wedges to stop their motion sickness.

item.title: The Xanadu Effect
item.description: What happens when we build big? Julia Barton remembers going to the top floor of Dallas’s then-new city hall when she was teenager. The building, designed by I.M. Pei, is a huge trapezoid jutting out over a wide plaza. Julia … Continue reading →

item.title: The Colour of Money
item.description: US paper currency is so ubiquitous that to really look at its graphic design with fresh eyes requires some deliberate and focused attention. So pull out a greenback from your wallet (or look at a picture one online) and just … Continue reading →

item.title: The Best Beer in the World
item.description: If you’re a beer nerd, or have a friend who’s a beer nerd, you’ve heard of Belgian beers. Belgians take beer very seriously. Amongst the 200 Belgian breweries, there’s a very specific sub-type: Trappist beers.

item.title: Frozen Music
item.description: Goethe said, “Architecture is frozen music.” I like that. Of course that was before audio recording, so now, for the most part, music is frozen music. It’s only very recently in the history of music that we’ve been able to … Continue reading →

item.title: What Gave You That Idea
item.description: Starlee Kine’s friend Noel works in advertising. In 2003, Noel was working in at an agency in Richmond, VA. Everyone wanted to work on flashy spots like Apple or Nike or Gatorade. Do you know what wasn’t flashy? Insurance. Which … Continue reading →

item.title: Kickstarter Video for Season 3 of 99% Invisible
item.description: This is the Kickstarter video for funding the new season of 99% Invisible. If you enjoy the show and want to help keep it going, now is the time to go to our funding page and chip in a little. … Continue reading →

item.title: Some Other Sign that People Do Not Totally Regret Life
item.description: Sean Cole is a poet and he knows what you think of that. He is also a radio producer. One night, drunk and stumbling around the Hudson River with his friend Malissa O’Donnell, he discovered a monument — two of … Continue reading →

item.title: Names vs The Nothing
item.description: New Public Sites is an investigation into some of the invisible sites and overlooked features of our everyday public spaces. These are the liminal spaces within cities that are not traditionally framed as “public space” because, quite frankly,

item.title: Two Storeys
item.description: While we’re gearing up for season 3, we present two pieces from two shows we love: First up, Language Bites from RTE Choice in Ireland. Language Bites is a series of 1-minute programs exploring the origins of popular phrases in … Continue reading →

item.title: BackStory- Heyward Shepherd Memorial
item.description: I only recently started listening to BackStory with the American History Guys, but it’s already earned a top spot in my crowded weekly rotation. With great stories and lively discussion, the “History Guys” connect our history to the present day.

item.title: A Series of Tubes
item.description: Pneumatic (adj.): of, or pertaining to, air, gases, or wind. In the world before telephone, radio, and email, the tasks of transmitting information and moving material objects were essentially the same challenge.

item.title: Q2
item.description: Benjamen Walker had a theory that priority queues are changing the American experience of waiting in line. So he visited amusement parks, highways, and community colleges to find out how these priority queues work and who is using them.

item.title: The Political Stage
item.description: On this special edition of 99% Invisible, we joined forces with Andrea Seabrook of DecodeDC to investigate all the thought that goes into the most miniscule details of a political campaign. Andrea was the star of episode #48 of 99% … Continue reading →

item.title: Derelict Dome
item.description: In the Cape Cod town of Woods Hole, buildings are not usually dome-shaped. Producer Katie Klocksin was pretty surprised when she came across one. Katie started asking around about the dome. She found it was built by the late Buckminster … Continue rea...

item.title: Razzle Dazzle
item.description: When most people think of camouflage they think of blending in with the environment, but camouflage can also take the opposite approach. It has long been hypothesized that stripes on zebras make it difficult for a predator to distinguish one … Continue...

item.title: Kowloon Walled City
item.description: Kowloon Walled City was the densest place in the world, ever. By its peak in the 1990s, the 6.5 acre Kowloon Walled City was home to at least 33,000 people (with estimates of up to 50,000). That’s a population density … Continue reading →

item.description: I want you to conjure an image in your mind of the white stripes that divide the lanes of traffic going the same direction on a major highway. How long are the stripes and the spaces between them? You can … Continue reading →

item.title: The Brief and Tumultuous Life of the New UC Logo
item.description: If you’re not from California, or missed this bit of news, the University of California has a new logo. Or rather had a new logo. To be more precise they had a new “visual identity system,” which is the kind … Continue reading →

item.title: The Great Red Car Conspiracy
item.description: When Eric Molinsky lived in Los Angeles, he kept hearing this story about a bygone transportation system called the Red Car. The Red Car, he was told, had been this amazing network of streetcars that connected the city–until a car … Continue reading →

item.description: Though its officially name is JFK Plaza, the open space near Philadelphia’s City Hall is more commonly known as LOVE Park. With its sleek granite benches, geometric raised planter beds, and long expanses of pavement,

item.title: New Old Town
item.description: Like many cities in Central Europe, Warsaw is made up largely of grey, ugly, communist block-style architecture. Except for one part: The Old Town. Walking through this historic district, it’s just like any other quaint European city.

item.title: The Zanzibar and Other Building Poems
item.description: There comes a time in the life of a modern city where it begins to grow up–literally. Santiago, the capital of Chile, has been going through a tremendous growth spurt since its economic boom of the mid 1990s. It happened … Continue reading →

item.description: There was a time when every street sign, every billboard, and every window display was made by a sign artist with a paint kit and an arsenal of squirrel- or camel-hair brushes. Some lived an itinerant lifestyle, traveling from town … Continue reading →

item.title: Secret Staircases
item.description: Wherever there is sufficient demand to move between two points of differing elevation, there are stairs. In some hilly neighborhoods of California–if you know where to look–you’ll find public, outdoor staircases.

item.title: The Modern Moloch
item.description: On the streets of early 20th Century America, nothing moved faster than 10 miles per hour. Responsible parents would tell their children, “Go outside, and play in the streets. All day.” And then the automobile happened.

item.title: Game Changer
item.description: Regardless of how you feel about basketball, you’ve got to appreciate the way it can bring groups of strangers together to share moments of pure adulation and collective defeat. That moment when time is running out, the team is down … Continue reading →

item.title: No Armed Bandit
item.description: Americans have always had an uneasy relationship with gambling. To circumvent anti-gambling laws in the US, early slot machines masqueraded as vending machines. They gave out chewing gum as prizes, and those prizes could be redeemed for cash.

item.title: The Symphony of Sirens plus Soviet Design

item.description: Lawyers have an ethics code. Journalists have an ethics code. Architects do, too. According to Ethical Standard 1.4 of the American Institute of Architects (AIA): “Members should uphold human rights in all their professional endeavors.

item.title: Rebar and the Alvord Lake Bridge
item.description: There’s something about rebar that fascinates me. If nothing else because there are very few things that invoke a fear of being skewered. My preoccupation with metal reinforcement bars dovetails nicely with a structure in San Francisco I’ve kind of … C...

item.description: I’m willing to concede from the get-go that I might be wrong about the entire premise of this story, but Superman has never really worked for me as a character. I preferred the more grounded Marvel Comic book characters, like … Continue reading →

item.title: I Heart NY, TM

item.title: Bubble Houses

item.title: Wild Ones Live
item.description: We have one cardinal rule on 99% Invisible: No cardinals. Meaning, we deal with the built world, not the natural world. So, when I read Jon Mooallem’s brilliant book, Wild Ones: A Sometimes Dismaying, Weirdly Reassuring Story About Looking at … Continu...

item.title: Kickstart Season 4 of 99% Invisible- Weekly Episodes
item.description: 99% Invisible started as a side project I made in my bedroom at night, and after two years of making the program, I turned to Kickstarter to see if I should keep it going.

item.title: All the Buildings
item.description: I love those moments when you’re walking in your neighborhood and suddenly nothing is familiar. In a good way. Sean Cole began seeing his neighborhood, actually the whole city of New York, with new eyes because of one artist who … Continue reading →

item.title: Unbuilt
item.description: There is an allure in unbuilt structures: the utopian, futuristic transports, the impossibly tall skyscrapers, even the horrible highways, all capture our imagination with what could have been. Whether these never built structures are perceived as good...

item.title: Future Screens are Mostly Blue
item.description: We have seen the future, and the future is mostly blue. Or, put another way: in our representations of the future in science fiction movies, blue seems to be the dominant color of our interfaces with technology yet to come. … Continue reading →

item.title: DIY Space Suit
item.description: Cameron Smith is building a space suit in his apartment. He’s not an astronaut. He’s not even an engineer. Cameron Smith is an archaeologist–on faculty in the anthropology department at Portland State University in Oregon.

item.title: Numbers Stations
item.description: If you tune around on a shortwave radio, you might stumble across a voice reciting an endless stream of numbers. Just numbers, all day, everyday. These so-called “numbers stations,” say nothing about where they are transmitting from or who they … Conti...

item.title: Higher And Higher
item.description: Like the best of these stories, the two bitter rivals started out as best friends: William Van Alen and Craig Severance. They were business partners. Van Alen was considered the artistic maverick and Severance was the savvy businessman.

item.title: Cover Story

item.description: There is a beauty to a universal standard. The idea that people across the world can agree that when they interact with one specific thing, everyone will be on the same page– regardless of language or culture or geographic locale. … Continue reading →

item.title: The Fancy Shape
item.description: Quatrefoil is the name of the four-lobed cloverleaf shape. It’s everywhere: adorning Gothic cathedrals, more modern churches, Rhode Island mansions, mission-style roofs in California, and decorating victorian homes from coast to coast.

item.title: Call Now
item.description: When it’s three o’clock in the morning and everything is going wrong in your life, there’s a certain kind of ad you might see on basic cable. Lawyers–usually guys–promise to battle the heartless, tight-wad insurance companies on your behalf.

item.title: Barcodes

item.title: A Sea Worth its Salt
item.description: The largest body of water in California was formed by a mistake. In 1905, the California Development Company accidentally flooded a huge depression in the Sonora Desert, creating an enormous salty lake called the Salton Sea.

item.title: Photo Credit
item.description: Founded by architect Walter Gropius in 1919, the Bauhaus school in Germany would go on to shape modern architecture, art, and design for decades to come. The school sought to combine design and industrialization,

item.title: On Average
item.description: In many ways, the built world was not designed for you. It was designed for the average person. Standardized tests, building codes, insurance rates, clothing sizes, The Dow Jones – all these measurements are based around the concept of an … Continue re...

item.title: Public Works
item.description: Infrastructure makes modern civilization possible. Roads, power grids, sewage systems and water networks all underpin society as we know it, forming the basis of our built environment … at least when they work.

item.title: Making Up Ground
item.description: Large portions of San Francisco, New York City, Boston, Seattle, Hong Kong and Marseilles were built on top of human made land. What is now Mumbai, India, was transformed by the British from a seven-island archipelago to one contiguous strip … Continue...

item.title: The Trend Forecast
item.description: Who decides that the color this season is “mint green” or that denim jackets are “back?” Of course, there’s top-down fashion, where couture houses and runway shows set a trend that trickles down through the rest of the industry. Then … Continue reading →

item.title: Longbox (Repeat)
item.description: Reporter Whitney Jones argues that R.E.M.’s Out of Time is the most politically significant album in the history of the United States. Because of its packaging. Longbox Please Vote.

item.title: Project Cybersyn
item.description: On September 11, 1973, a military junta violently took control of Chile, which was led at the time by President Salvador Allende. Allende had become president in a free and democratic election. After the military coup,

item.title: Half a House
item.description: On the night of February 27th, 2010, a magnitude of 8.8 earthquake hit Constitución, Chile and it was the second biggest that the world had seen in half a century. The quake and the tsunami it produced completely crushed the … Continue reading →

item.title: McMansion Hell
item.description: Few forms of contemporary architecture draw as much criticism as the McMansion, a particular type of oversized house that people love to hate. McMansions usually feature 3,000 or more square feet of space and fail to embody a cohesive style … Continue ...

item.title: Space Trash, Space Treasure
item.description: In the summer of 1961 the upper stage of the rocket carrying the Transit 4A satellite blew up about two hours after launch. It was the first known human-made object to unintentionally explode in space, and it created hundreds of … Continue reading →

item.title: The Shift
item.description: Every now and again, a truly great athlete shatters all previous assumptions about what’s possible to achieve in a sport. When this happens, opposing teams scramble to find ways to stop them or slow them down. In basketball,

item.title: Ten Letters for the President
item.description: People who write the White House know that the president himself will most likely not see their message. Many of their letters start with phrases like, “I know no one will read this.” Although someone does read those letters. And … Continue reading →

item.title: Reverb
item.description: Through a combination of passive and active acoustics, architects and acousticians can control the sounds of spaces to fit any kind of need. With sound-proofing and selective-amplification, we can add reverb or take it away.

item.title: Dollar Store Town
item.description: Dollar stores are not just a U.S. phenomenon. They can be found in Australia and the United Kingdom, the Middle East and Mexico. And a lot of the stuff—the generic cheap stuff for sale in these stores—comes from one place. … Continue reading →

item.title: NBC Chimes
item.description: The NBC chimes may be the most famous sound in broadcasting. Originating in the 1920s, the three key sequential notes are familiar to generations of radio listeners and television watchers. Many companies have tried to trademark sounds but only around ...

item.title: Guano Island
item.description: In 2014, President Obama expanded the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, making it the largest marine preserve in the world at the time. The expansion closed 490,000 square miles of largely undisturbed ocean to commercial fishing and unde...

item.title: Plat of Zion
item.description: The urban grid of Salt Lake City, Utah is designed to tell you exactly where you are in relation to Temple Square, one of the holiest sites for Mormons. Addresses can read like sets of coordinates. “300 South 2100 East,” … Continue reading →

item.title: Mini-Stories: Volume 1
item.description: Host Roman Mars talks to the 99pi producers about their favorite “Mini-Stories.” These are little anecdotes or seeds of a story about design and architecture that can’t quite stretch into a full episode, but the staff loves them anyway.

item.title: Mini-Stories: Volume 2
item.description: Part 2 where host Roman Mars talks to the 99pi producers about their favorite “Mini-Stories.” These are little anecdotes or seeds of a story about design and architecture that can’t quite stretch into a full episode, but we love them … Continue reading →

item.title: Tom Swift and His Electric Rifle
item.description: On January 3, 1979, two officers from the Los Angeles Police Department went to the home of Eulia May Love, a 39-year-old African-American mother. The police were there because of a dispute over an unpaid gas bill.

item.title: The Revolutionary Post
item.description: Winifred Gallagher, author of How the Post Office Created America: A History, argues that the post office is not simply an inexpensive way to send a letter. The service was designed to unite a bunch of disparate towns and people … Continue reading →

item.title: The Eponymist
item.description: Eponym (noun): A person after whom a discovery, invention, place, etc., is named or thought to be named; a name or noun formed after a person. An eponym, almost by definition, has some kind of story behind it — some reason it … Continue reading →

item.title: Usonia 1
item.description: Frank Lloyd Wright was a bombastic character that ultimately changed the field of architecture, and not just through his big, famous buildings. Before designing many of his most well-known works, Wright created a small and inexpensive yet beautiful hou...

item.title: Usonia the Beautiful
item.description: Frank Lloyd Wright believed that the buildings we live in shape the kinds of people we become. His aim was nothing short of rebuilding the entire culture of the United States, changing the nation through its architecture.

item.title: Atom in the Garden of Eden
item.description: As the world entered the Atomic Age, humankind faced a new fear that permeated just about every aspect of daily life: the threat of nuclear war. And while the violent applications of atomic research had already been proven,

item.title: Church (Sanctuary, Part 1)
item.description: In the 1980s, Rev. John Fife and his congregation at Southside Presbyterian Church began to help Central American migrants fleeing persecution from US backed dictatorships. Their efforts would mark the beginning of a new — and controversial — social mo...

item.title: State (Sanctuary, Part 2)
item.description: In the 1980s, the United States experienced a refugee crisis. Thousands of Central Americans were fleeing civil wars in El Salvador and Guatemala, traveling north through Mexico, and crossing the border into the U.S. [Note: Just tuning in?

item.title: Negative Space: Logo Design with Michael Bierut
item.description: Logos used to be a thing people didn’t really give much thought to. But over the last decade, the volume and intensity of arguments about logos have increased substantially. A lot of this is just the internet being the internet. … Continue reading →

item.title: The Falling of the Lenins
item.description: On the night of December 8, 2013, a huge crowd gathered on a tree-lined boulevard in downtown Kiev, Ukraine. The crowd was there to watch as a statue in the boulevard was pulled down by a crane. The toppled statue … Continue reading →

item.title: Manzanar
item.description: When Warren Furutani was growing up in Los Angeles in the 1950s, he sometimes heard his parents refer to a place where they once spent time — a place they called “camp.” To him “camp” meant summer camp or a … Continue reading →

item.title: Containers
item.description: We’re based in beautiful downtown Oakland, CA which is a port city in the San Francisco Bay. Massive container ships travel across the Pacific and end up here. From miles away you can see the enormous white cranes that pull … Continue reading →

item.title: The Architect of Hollywood
item.description: Los Angeles is rich with architectural diversity. On the same block, you could find a retro-futuristic Googie diner next to a Spanish-style mansion, sitting comfortably alongside a Dutch Colonial dwelling, all in close proximity to a Deconstructivist c...

item.title: Sounds Natural
item.description: In most wildlife films, the sounds you hear were not recorded while the cameras were rolling. Most filmmakers use long telephoto lenses to film animals, but there’s no sonic equivalent of a zoom lens. Good audio requires a microphone close … Continue r...

item.title: Reversing the Grid
item.description: For most people, electricity only flows one way (into the home), but there are exceptions — people who use solar panels, for instance. In those cases, excess electricity created by the solar cells travels back out into the grid to … Continue reading →

item.title: The Modern Necropolis
item.description: In the town of Colma, California, the dead outnumber the living by a thousand to one. Located just ten miles south of San Francisco, Colma is filled with rolling green hills, manicured hedges, and 17 full size cemeteries (18 if … Continue reading →

item.title: This Is Chance: Anchorwoman of the Great Alaska Earthquake
item.description: This episode was recorded live as part of the Radiotopia West Coast Tour. It was the middle of the night on March 27, 1964. Earlier that evening, the second-biggest earthquake ever measured at the time had hit Anchorage, Alaska. 115 people died.

item.title: New Jersey
item.description: The Brazilian soccer shirt is iconic. Its bright canary yellow with green trim, worn with blue shorts, is known worldwide. The uniform is joyful and bold and seems to capture something essential about Brazil.

item.title: Squatters of the Lower East Side
item.description: In 1987, three years after moving to New York City, Maggie Wrigley found herself on the edge of homelessness. She was trying to figure out where to stay, when she heard about an abandoned tenement building on the Lower East … Continue reading →

item.title: The Yin and Yang of Basketball (Repeat)
item.description: In 1891, a physical education teacher in Springfield, Massachusetts invented the game we would come to know as basketball. In setting the height of the baskets, he inadvertently created a design problem that would not be resolved for decades to … Conti...

item.title: Intro to a new Roman Mars podcast: What Trump Can Teach Us About Con Law
item.description: Special introductory episode to a new podcast produced by Roman Mars and Elizabeth Joh. Professor Elizabeth Joh teaches Intro to Constitutional Law and most of the time this is a pretty straight forward job. But with Trump in office,

item.title: In the Same Ballpark
item.description: In the 1992, the Baltimore Orioles opened their baseball season at a brand new stadium called Oriole Park at Camden Yards, right along the downtown harbor. The stadium was small and intimate, built with brick and iron trusses—a throwback to … Continue ...

item.title: You Should Do a Story
item.description: “You should do a story…” is the first line to a lot of the conversations you have when you work at 99pi. This week we look into a bunch of those stories suggested by our listeners and present them to … Continue reading →

item.title: Mexico 68
item.description: The 1968 Olympics took place in Mexico City, Mexico. It was the first Games ever hosted in a Latin American country. And for Mexico City, the event was an opportunity to show the world that they were a metropolis as worthy as London, Berlin, Rome or Tokyo to host this huge international affair.

item.title: The Pool and the Stream
item.description: This is the story of a curvy, kidney-shaped swimming pool born in Northern Europe that had a huge ripple effect on popular culture in Southern California and landscape architecture in Northern California, and then the world.

item.title: Repackaging the Pill
item.description: Most people are familiar with at least one version of the birth control pill’s packaging — a round plastic disc which opens like a shell and looks like a makeup compact. But the pill wasn’t always packaged this way. The … Continue reading →

item.title: The Trials of Dan and Dave
item.description: This is the story of an ad campaign produced for the 1992 Olympic games in Barcelona. Perennial runner-up in the sports shoe category, Reebok, was trying to make its mark and take down Nike. They chose two athletes, plucked them … Continue reading →

item.title: El Gordo
item.description: In Spain, they do the lottery differently. First of all, it’s a country-wide obsession — about 75% of Spaniards buy a ticket. There’s more than one lottery in Spain, but the one that Spaniards are the most passionate about is … Continue reading →

item.title: Ways of Hearing
item.description: When the tape started rolling in old analog recording studios, there was a feeling that musicians were about to capture a particular moment. On tape, there was no “undo.” They could try again, if they had the time and money, … Continue reading →

item.title: The Stethoscope
item.description: Imagine for a moment the year 1800. A doctor is meeting with a patient – most likely in the patient’s home. The patient is complaining about shortness of breath. A cough, a fever. The doctor might check the patient’s pulse … Continue reading →

item.title: The Great Dismal Swamp
item.description: On the border of Virginia and North Carolina stretches a great, dismal swamp. The Great Dismal Swamp, actually — that’s the name British colonists gave it centuries ago. The swamp covers about 190 square miles today, but at its peak, … Continue reading →

item.title: Person in Lotus Position
item.description: Tech analysts estimate that over six billion emojis are sent each day. Emojis, which started off as a collection of low-resolution pixelated images from Japan, have become a well-established and graphically sophisticated part of everyday global communi...

item.title: Notes on an Imagined Plaque
item.description: Monuments don’t just appear in the wake of someone’s death — they are erected for reasons specific to a time and place. In 1905, one such memorial was put up in downtown Memphis, Tennessee, to commemorate Nathan Bedford Forrest, who … Continue reading →

item.title: The Age of the Algorithm
item.description: Computer algorithms now shape our world in profound and mostly invisible ways. They predict if we’ll be valuable customers and whether we’re likely to repay a loan. They filter what we see on social media, sort through resumes,

item.title: Coal Hogs Work Safe
item.description: Coal miner stickers started out as little advertisements that the manufacturers of mining equipment handed out. Even before the late 1960s, when mining safety laws started requiring reflective materials underground,

item.title: The Finnish Experiment
item.description: Around the world, there is a lot of buzz around the idea of universal basic income (also known as “unconditional basic income” or UBI). It can take different forms or vary in the details, but in essence: UBI is the … Continue reading →

item.title: Ponte City Tower
item.description: Ponte City Tower, the brutalist cylindrical high-rise that towers over Johannesburg, has gone from a symbol of white opulence to something far more complicated. It’s gone through very hard times, but also it’s hopeful.

item.title: The Athletic Brassiere
item.description: Among the most important advances in sports technology, few can compete with the invention of the sports bra. Following the passage of Title IX in 1972, women’s interest in athletics surged. But their breasts presented an obstacle.

item.title: The Containment Plan
item.description: It’s hard to overstate the vastness of the Skid Row neighborhood in Los Angeles. It spans roughly 50 blocks, which is about a fifth of the entire downtown area of Los Angeles. It’s very clear when you’ve entered Skid Row.

item.title: Half Measures
item.description: The United States is one of just a handful of countries that that isn’t officially metric. Instead, Americans measure things our own way, in units that are basically inscrutable to non-Americans, nearly all of whom have been brought up in … Continue re...

item.title: La Sagrada Familia
item.description: The line to enter Barcelona’s most famous cathedral often stretches around the block. La Sagrada Família, designed by Antoni Gaudí, draws millions of visitors each year. There are a lot of Gothic churches in Spain, but this one is different.

item.title: Oyster-tecture
item.description: New York was built at the mouth of the Hudson River and one creature in particular shaped the landscape: the oyster. Over time, pollution and other environmental changes killed off that oyster population, but a new underwater landscape architecture project aims to bring them back.

item.title: Dollhouses of St. Louis
item.description: People have been stealing the bricks of St. Louis.

item.title: Hero Props: Graphic Design in Film & Television
item.description: Annie Atkins specializes in graphics for filmmaking, including lettering, illustrations and more -- she has designed all kinds of graphic props, including telegrams, vintage cigarette packaging, maps, love letters, books, passports and fake CIA identification cards.

item.title: Money Makers
item.description: For a long time, anti-counterfeiting laws made it illegal to show US currency in movies. Now you can show real money, but fake money is often preferred. Creating fake money that looks real enough for film is a tough design challenge.

item.title: A 700-Foot Mountain of Whipped Cream
item.description: Clive Desmond covers the golden age of radio ads, featuring Frank Zappa, Ken Nordine, Linda Ronstadt, and Randy Newman in this featured episode.

item.title: The Nut Behind the Wheel
item.description: The culture of heavily regulated, data-driven, auto-safety engineering did not always exist. In fact, for decades, automakers tried to keep data about car wrecks to themselves. They not only resisted making cars safer, they argued the very idea of a “safe car” was impossible.

item.title: Guerrilla Public Service Redux
item.description: In the early morning of August 5, 2001, artist Richard Ankrom and a group of friends assembled on the 4th Street bridge over the 110 freeway in Los Angeles. They had gathered to commit a crime — one Ankrom had plotted for years.

item.title: Mini-Stories: Volume 3
item.description: Mysterious ice boats, green ruins, sack dresses, steampunk violins, and a little update from a couple of the notable city flags that have been redesigned around the country.

item.title: Biomimicry- Vox + 99% Invisible Video
item.description: Japan’s Shinkansen doesn’t look like your typical train. It is a fascinating example of biomimicry, the design movement pioneered by biologist and writer Janine Benyus.

item.title: Mini-Stories: Volume 4
item.description: This part two of the 2017/2018 mini-stories episodes, where Roman interviews the staff and our collaborators about their favorite little design stories that don’t quite fill out an entire episode.

item.title: Thermal Delight
item.description: Air conditioning does a lot more than cool spaces. It has dramatically changed where people in the United States live and the design of homes and other buildings.

item.title: Speech Bubbles: Understanding Comics with Scott McCloud
item.description: Cartoonist and theorist Scott McCloud has been making and thinking about comics for decades. He is the author of Understanding Comics: The Invisible Art. This classic volume explores formal aspects of comics, the historical development of the medium, its fundamental vocabulary, and various ways in which these elements have been used.

item.title: Managed Retreat
item.description: In the 1970s it looked like the beloved, 200-year-old Cape Hatteras lighthouse was in danger. The sea was getting closer and threatening to swallow it up. And people were torn over what to do about it -- they could move the lighthouse, or leave it in place and try to defend it against the forces of nature. For the next 30 years, the locals fought an intense political battle over this decision. It’s the kind of battle we can expect to see a lot more of as sea levels rise and threaten coastal communities around the world.

item.title: Border Wall
item.description: Three stories about the physical border at the southern edge of the U.S.

item.title: Making a Mark: Visual Identity with Tom Geismar
item.description: Tom Geismar has been a driving force in the field of design and graphic identity for over 60 years. The influence of the firm he co-founded can be felt in logos you see every day.

item.title: Bijlmer (City of the Future, Part 1)
item.description: After World War 2, city planners in Amsterdam wanted to design the perfect “City of the Future.” They decided to build a new neighborhood, close to Amsterdam, that would be a perfect encapsulation of Modernist principles. It was called the Bijlmermeer, and it tested the lofty ideas of the International Congress of Modern Architecture on a grand scale. When it was over, no one would ever try it again.

item.title: Blood, Sweat and Tears (City of the Future, Part 2)
item.description: The story of the Bijlmer continues

item.title: Fordlandia
item.description: In the late 1920s, the Ford Motor Company bought up millions of acres of land in Brazil. They loaded boats with machinery and supplies, and shipped them deep into the Amazon rainforest. Workers cut down trees and cleared the land and then they built a rubber plantation in the middle of one of the wildest places on earth.

item.title: Miss Manhattan Redux
item.description: All around the country, there stands a figure so much a part of historical architecture and urban landscapes that she is rarely noticed.

item.title: Gerrymandering
item.description: The way we draw our political districts has a huge effect on U.S. politics, but the process is also greatly misunderstood. Gerrymandering has become a scapegoat for what’s wrong with the polarized American political system, blamed for marginalizing groups and rigging elections, but there’s no simple, one-size-fits-all design solution for drawing fair districts.

item.title: Airships and the Future that Never Was
item.description: They are hulking, but graceful -- human-made whales that float in the air. For over a century, lighter-than-air vehicles have captured the public imagination, playing a recurring role in our dreams of alternate realities and futures that might have been.

item.title: Making it Rain
item.description: The battlefield has always been at the mercy of the climate, but there was a time in U.S. military history when we did more than just pray for advantageous weather. We tried to create it.

item.title: Lessons from Las Vegas
item.description: To this day, architects tend to turn their noses up at Las Vegas, or simply dismiss it as irrelevant to serious design theory.

item.title: The Hair Chart
item.description: Andre Walker became famous for being Oprah Winfrey’s hair stylist, but he is also known for something else: a system that he created back in the 1990s to market his line of hair care products. The system categorizes natural hair types, and it's often referred to simply as "the hair chart."

item.title: Gander International Airport
item.description: The Gander Airport in Newfoundland was once the easternmost airfield in North America, so when transatlantic air travel was new and difficult, Gander played in critical role in getting people back and forth from Europe to America.

item.title: The Laff Box
item.description: For nearly five decades, the laugh track was ubiquitous on television sitcoms, but in the early 2000s, it began to disappear. What happened?

item.title: Breaking Bad News
item.description: When a doctor reveals a terminal diagnosis to a patient -- that process is as delicate a procedure as any surgery, with potentially serious consequences if things go wrong.

item.title: Immobile Homes
item.description: About a third of mobile homeowners live in parks where they rent a plot of land for their home. This arrangement is filled with uncertainty.

item.title: Curb Cuts
item.description: If you live in an American city and you don’t personally use a wheelchair, it's easy to overlook the small ramp at most intersections, between the sidewalk and the street. Today, these curb cuts are everywhere.

item.title: The Vault
item.description: Svalbard is a remote Norwegian archipelago that's the home to a vault containing seeds for virtually every edible plant one can imagine.

item.title: 77 Steps
item.description: As the U.S. war effort ramped up in the early 1940s, the Navy put out a request for chair design submissions. They needed a chair that was fireproof, waterproof, lightweight and strong enough to survive a torpedo blast. In response, engineer named Wilton C. Dinges designed a chair made out of aluminum, bent and welded to be super strong.

item.title: The Barney Design
item.description: Until the early 90s, basketball uniforms were pretty tame. There had been real limits to what could be done with jerseys. All the details—the numbers, the names, the logos—had to be sewed on. Complicated graphics would have taken a massive amount of embroidery, which would have added additional weight and made the jersey hotter to wear. But dye sublimation changed everything.

item.title: Post-Narco Urbanism
item.description: In the 1980s, Pablo Escobar, the notorious drug lord, had effectively declared war on the Colombian state. The bloodshed was focused in the city of Medellin. As the years went on, Medellin became the most dangerous city in the world. But today, Medellin is very different.

item.title: Right to Roam
item.description: In the United Kingdom, the freedom to walk through private land is known as “the right to roam.” The movement to win this right was started in the 1930s by a rebellious group of young people who called themselves “ramblers” and spent their days working in the factories of Manchester, England.

item.title: VIDEO- Why Danger Symbols Can't Last Forever with Vox
item.description: The world is full of icons that warn us to be afraid — to stay away from this or not do that. And many of these are easy to understand because they represent something recognizable, like a fire, or a person slipping on a wet floor. But some concepts are hard to communicate visually.

item.title: Roman Mars on ZigZag
item.description: A special presentation of Radiotopia's newest show ZigZag

item.title: Interrobang
item.description: In 1962, an ad man decided that excited and exclamatory questions needed their own end punctuation: the iterrobang

item.title: Everything is Alive
item.description: Everything is Alive is an unscripted interview show with host Ian Chillag in which all the subjects are inanimate objects. In each episode, a different thing tells us its life story -- and everything it says is true.

item.title: The Shipping Forecast
item.description: Four times every day, on radios all across the British Isles, a BBC announcer begins reading from a seemingly indecipherable script. "And now the Shipping Forecast..."

item.title: Built to Burn
item.description: After the massive Panorama Fire in southern California in 1980, a young fire researcher named Jack Cohen went in to investigate the houses that were destroyed and he found something surprising

item.title: Fire and Rain
item.description: For Montecito, California, fire is predictable and it is inevitable. Now, coupled with multi-year drought, it is becoming unmanageable.

item.title: It's Chinatown
item.description: For Americans, the sight of pagoda roofs and dragon gates means that you are in Chinatown. Whether in San Francisco, New York, Los Angeles, or Las Vegas, the chinoiserie look is distinctive. But for people from China, the Chinatown aesthetic can feel surprisingly foreign. The same goes for fortune cookies.

item.title: Bundyville
item.description: The first episode in a fascinating series about the fight over federal land in the western US and the family at the center of it.

item.title: Double Standards
item.description: Blepharoplasty is often done to lift loose or sagging skin around the upper eyelids caused by aging. But for a lot of people of Asian descent, this surgery is not strictly about aging and more commonly referred to as “double eyelid” surgery.

item.title: The First Straw
item.description: A straw is a simple thing. It’s a tube, a conveyance mechanism for liquid. The defining characteristic of the straw is the emptiness inside it. This is the stuff of tragedy, and America.

item.title: The House that Came in the Mail
item.description: The Sears catalog tells the tale of a world -- itemized. And starting in 1908, the company that offered America everything began offering what just might be its most audacious product line ever: houses.

item.title: Billboard Boys: The Greatest Radio Contest of All Time
item.description: A radio contest offering a $18,000 modular home to the person who will live on a billboard platform the longest gets out of control

item.title: Kids' Clothes: Articles of Interest #1
item.description: Articles of Interest is a show about what we wear: a six-part series looking clothing within 99% Invisible. Episode 1: Kids' Clothes

item.title: Plaid: Articles of Interest #2
item.description: Lots of different groups have adopted plaid over the course of the 20th century, but if we want to explore how this pattern proliferated, we’ve got to go to Scotland.

item.title: Pockets: Articles of Interest #3
item.description: Womenswear is littered with fake pockets that don’t open, or shallow pockets that can hardly hold more than a paperclip. If women's clothes have pockets at all, they are often and smaller and just fit less than men’s pockets do.

item.title: Hawaiian Shirts: Articles of Interest #4
item.description: Much of understanding an aloha shirt is about paying attention to what is on the shirt itself. It’s about looking at the pattern to see the story it tells.

item.title: Blue Jeans: Articles of Interest #5
item.description: You can learn a lot from observing an old pair of blue jeans.

item.title: Punk Style: Articles of Interest #6
item.description: Groups of people who historically haven’t had a voice, have expressed themselves on their bodies. Through their style, their hair, their tattoos, their piercings, and what they wear.

item.title: The Worst Way to Start a City
item.description: Sam Anderson, author of Boom Town, guides us through the chaotic founding of Oklahoma City, which happened all in one day in 1889

item.title: Welcome to Jurassic Art
item.description: The illustrated interpretation of dinosaur morphology and behavior has had a big impact on how the public views dinosaurs and it's gone through a couple of key turning points, including a more recent push for more speculative paleoart.

item.title: A Year in the Dark
item.description: The almost year-long struggle to get power working on the island and the utility worker who became a Puerto Rican folk hero.

item.title: Devolutionary Design
item.description: Devo’s first record and the fight over the arresting image of a flashy, handsome golf legend on the cover.

item.title: Orphan Drugs
item.description: The fight to get drugs that treat rare diseases on the market and the unintended consequence of that fight, which affected the cost of all kinds of drugs.

item.title: The Green Book redux
item.description: Two stories of the Green Book guide to Negro Motorists...not currently playing in a theater near you

item.title: Raccoon Resistance
item.description: After Toronto unveiled its "raccoon-resistant" compost bins in 2016, some people feared the animals would be starved but many more celebrated the innovative design. Rolling out this novel locked bin opened a new battlefront in city's ongoing "war on raccoons."

item.title: Oñate's Foot
item.description: A years long fight in New Mexico over the best way to commemorate a "founding father" with a cruel past.

item.title: The Accidental Room
item.description: We explore (and live inside!) the fascinating public/private space known as "the mall."

item.title: Bonus Episode- Avery talks Articles of Interest with Roman
item.description: Roman talks with Avery about the lessons learned from making Articles of Interest

item.title: Mini-Stories: Volume 5
item.description: The 99pi crew tells short stories to the delight of Roman Mars

item.title: Christmas with The Allusionist
item.description: For the holidays this year, we're presenting a two-part Radiotopia feature with friend of the show (and host of The Allusionist podcast) Helen Zaltzman, each tackling a different aspect of this festive season.

item.title: Gathering the Magic
item.description: The magic of Magic with Eric Molinsky of Imaginary Worlds

item.title: Mini-Stories: Volume 6
item.description: The sixth installment of our staff mini stories: mythical alleys, karaoke, burning monsters, time thieves, and where Canadians pick up their Amazon packages.

item.title: Atomic Tattoos
item.description: Atomic tattoos and concept of survivability. The idea that with enough canned food, shelters, fearlessness (and maybe tattoos) the American people would be able to survive an atomic attack.

item.title: Crude Habitat
item.description: What should we do with old infrastructure that has been incorporated into the natural ecosystem?

item.title: The Tunnel
item.description: In May of 1990, law enforcement raided a warehouse in Douglas, AZ and a private home across the border in Agua Prieta, Mexico.

item.title: The Secret Lives of Color
item.description: Throughout human history we've been driven to find the brightest splash of color

item.title: National Sword
item.description: The biggest buyer of recycling just stopped buying

item.title: Beneath the Ballpark
item.description: The Dodgers needed a home in LA, but the home they chose already belonged to someone else

item.title: Usonia Redux
item.description: Frank Lloyd Wright changed the field of architecture, and not just through his big, famous buildings

item.title: The Known Unknown
item.description: The tradition of the Tomb of the Unknown goes back only about a century, but it has become one of the most solemn and reverential monuments.

item.title: Classic Cartoon Sound Effects!
item.description: Cartoon sound effects are some of the most iconic sounds ever made. Even modern cartoons continue to use the same sound effects from decades ago.

item.title: Palaces for the People
item.description: Social Infrastructure is the glue that binds communities together

item.title: The Many Deaths of a Painting
item.description: When Barnett Newman’s painting Who’s Afraid of Red, Yellow and Blue III was placed in the Sedelijk museum it was meant to be provocative, but one reaction that it received was so intense, so violent, it set off a chain of events that shook the art world to its core.

item.title: Three Things That Made the Modern Economy
item.description: 50 Things That Made The Modern Economy is a podcast that explores the fascinating histories of a number of powerful inventions and their far-reaching consequences.

item.title: Froebel's Gifts
item.description: If you’ve ever looked at a piece of abstract art or Modernist architecture and thought “my kindergartener could have made that," well, that may be more true than you realize.

item.title: The Roman Mars Mazda Virus
item.description: Reply All orchestrated a grand podcast crossover event to try to solve a years old bug plaguing 99% Invisible listeners

item.title: Play Mountain
item.description: The ideas behind Noguchi’s Play Mountain - about imagination, and freedom to play - have left a deep mark on playground designers, and are shaping the emerging playgrounds all around us.

item.title: Uptown Squirrel
item.description: Squirrels were purposefully introduced into our cities in the 1800s, and when their population exploded, we lost track of how many there are.

item.title: From Bombay with Love
item.description: The very deliberate cultural exchange program that made Soviets into some of Bollywood's biggest fans

item.title: Weeding is Fundamental
item.description: The fight over weeding books from the library

item.title: Sound and Health: Cities
item.description: How sound can be designed to reduce harm and even improve wellbeing

item.title: La Sagrada Familia (Repeat)
item.description: The line to enter Barcelona’s most famous cathedral often stretches around the block. La Sagrada Família, designed by Antoni Gaudí, draws millions of visitors each year. There are a lot of Gothic churches in Spain, but this one is different.

item.title: Sound and Health: Hospitals
item.description: Sound can have serious impacts on our health and wellbeing. And there’s no better place to think about health than hospitals.

item.title: Depave Paradise
item.description: Mexico City is in a water crisis. Despite rains and floods, it is running out of drinking water and the city is sinking

item.title: The Automat
item.description: Restaurants trying to cut back on the number of employees are using new technology to order and prepare food, but the "automated" restaurant has been tried before.

item.title: The Barney Design redux
item.description: The Raptors are in the NBA finals and their 90s uniforms are more popular than ever

item.title: The Anthropocene Reviewed
item.description: John Green rates different facets of the human-centered planet on a five-star scale

item.title: Life and Death in Singapore
item.description: In Singapore, where land is scarce, it’s not unlikely for apartment buildings to be built on top of land that was graveyards not too long ago. But building on top of a graveyard has its complications.

item.title: The Universal Page
item.description: How blind people experience literature and the long history of designing a tactile language that sometimes suffered from trying to be too universal

item.title: Built on Sand
item.description: Sand is the most important solid substance in the world...and we're running out

item.title: Goodness Gracious Great Balls of Twine
item.description: Vivian Le is on a mission that requires equal parts science, philosophy, and daring, in search of something that’s been hotly contested for decades: the world's largest ball of twine.

item.title: Invisible Women
item.description: Men are often the default subjects of design, which can have a huge impact on big and critical aspects of everyday life.

item.title: He's Still Neutral
item.description: The Buddha of Oakland

item.title: On Beeing
item.description: Artificial beehives had a huge impact on US agriculture

item.title: Model City
item.description: Using a massive scale model of the city of San Francisco built in the 1930s to tell the story of the city today

item.title: Peace Lines
item.description: The walls in Northern Ireland meant to separate the Catholic and Protestant neighborhoods

item.title: All Rings Considered
item.description: The meteoric rise and fall of the custom ringtone

item.title: Wait Wait...Tell Me!
item.description: Design can completely change whether a five minute wait feels reasonable or completely unbearable

item.title: The Pool and the Stream Redux
item.description: This is the UPDATED story of a curvy, kidney-shaped swimming pool born in Northern Europe that had a huge ripple effect on popular culture in Southern California and landscape architecture in Northern California, and then the world.

item.title: Dead Cars
item.description: Counting the dead cars in Bethel, AK

item.title: 99% Invisible presents What Trump Can Teach Us About Con Law
item.description: Learning the US Constitution through Trump tweets

item.title: The Help-Yourself City
item.description: All the ways people try to change their community that isn’t through city planning or some kind of official process.

item.title: The Kirkbride Plan
item.description: Kirkbrides and the architecture of madness

item.title: Unsure Footing
item.description: The little rule in soccer that fundamentally changed the game

item.title: Audio Guide to the Imperfections of a Perfect Masterpiece
item.description: To help celebrate its 60th anniversary, the Guggenheim Museum teamed up with 99% Invisible to offer visitors a guided audio experience of the museum

item.title: Great Bitter Lake Association
item.description: A little-known bit of world history about a rag tag group of sailors stranded at the center of a war.

item.title: How To Pick A Pepper
item.description: It is hard to pick a pepper, for both humans and robots

item.title: Ubiquitous Icons: Peace, Power, and Happiness
item.description: The stories behind graphic symbols you see every day

item.title: Cautionary Tales
item.description: Tim Harford takes us through all of the poor design choices leading into the infamous La La Land/Moonlight debacle, and how it could have been prevented.

item.title: Mannequin Pixie Dream Girl
item.description: Lester Gaba and his companion Cynthia were the talk of the town, but Cynthia wasn't a person, she was a new style of mannequin

item.title: The Infantorium
item.description: Incubator baby shows were a huge hit in early 20th century amusement parks and they were the main source of healthcare for premature babies for over forty years

item.title: The ELIZA Effect
item.description: What happens when computers master human language?

item.title: Smart Stuff with Justin and Roman- Founder Effect
item.description: A Smart, Erudite Podcast

item.title: Mini-Stories: Volume 7
item.description: Our annual mini-stories extravaganza, volume 7

item.title: Mini-Stories: Volume 8
item.description: Part 2 of our annual mini-stories extravaganza, volume 8

item.title: Shade
item.description: Shade is not evenly distributed in our cities and it's a matter of life and death

item.title: Their Dark Materials
item.description: Vantablack was created by the tech industry for the tech industry, but this strange dark material would also go on to turn the art world on its head.

item.title: The Worst Video Game Ever
item.description: Deep within the National Museum of American History’s vaults is a battered Atari case containing what’s known as “the worst video game of all time.”

item.title: Missing the Bus
item.description: A very old transit technology may be the key to making our cities better for everyone

item.title: Whomst Among Us Has Let The Dogs Out
item.description: The decade-long quest to find out Who Let The Dogs Out

item.title: Fraktur
item.description: The long and strange history of fraktur and its somewhat ironic designation as the "Nazi font"

item.title: Over the Road
item.description: The trials and triumphs of America’s long haul truckers

item.title: The Weather Machine
item.description: The massive interconnected network of technologies that span decades that come together to predict the weather

item.title: Map Quests: Political, Physical and Digital
item.description: Three stories of maps shaping the real world

item.title: Roman Mars Describes Things As They Are
item.description: On this shelter-in-place edition of 99pi, Roman walks around his house and tells stories about the history and design of various objects

item.title: This is Chance! Redux
item.description: A city shaken and a voice that held it together

item.title: This Day in Esoteric Political History
item.description: Roman talks with Jody Avirgan about his new show on Raditopia

item.title: Wipe Out
item.description: With the current shortage of toilet paper, we look at how it went from being a rarity to an essential item in just a few decades

item.title: Unsheltered in Place
item.description: Katie Mingle reports on how COVID-19 is affecting the unhoused and what people are doing to protect everyone

item.title: Masking for a Friend
item.description: Why the culture behind epidemic masks developed so differently in China and the doctor who started it all

item.title: The Smell of Concrete After Rain
item.description: Stories inspired by two people from the world of architecture and design we've lost due to COVID

item.title: The Natural Experiment
item.description: Researchers who are using this bizarre, tragic moment to learn something new about the world

item.title: A Fantasy of Fashion: Articles of Interest #7
item.description: In the wake of World War II, the government of France commissioned its most prominent designers to create a collection of miniature fashion dolls

item.title: Knockoffs: Articles of Interest #8
item.description: Brands hold immense sway over both consumers and the American legal system. Few know this as well as Dapper Dan.

item.title: Perfume: Articles of Interest #9
item.description: Perfume can be a key to a whole olfactory world

item.title: Suits: Articles of Interest #10
item.description: Formal menswear can be traced back to American Revolution, classical statuary, and one particular bloke bopping around downtown London way back in the 1770s.

item.title: Diamonds: Articles of Interest #11
item.description: Diamonds represent value, in all its multiple meanings: values, as in ethics, and value as in actual price. But what are these rocks actually worth?

item.title: Wedding Dresses: Articles of Interest #12
item.description: The symbolism and weight of the white wedding dress

item.title: Instant Gramification
item.description: How Instagram and architecture interact and feed back on each other

item.title: Return of the Yokai
item.description: In the US, mascots are used to pump up crowds at sporting events, or for traumatizing generations of children at Chuck E. Cheese, but in Japan it’s different.

item.title: Return of Oñate's Foot
item.description: All across the country, protestors have been challenging old monuments, including the ones of Juan de Oñate in NM, which we've been following for a couple years

item.title: Freedom House Ambulance Service
item.description: The forgotten story of the first paramedics in the US, a team of pioneering EMTs from Pittsburgh's largely Black Hill District

item.title: A Side of Franchise
item.description: Marcia Chatelain on how McDonald's replaced the state in Black America

item.title: The Dolphin that Roared
item.description: The story of a small island nation, a fight for independence, a fight for dependence...and a flag. Plus, we're announcing our first book!

item.title: Valley of the Fallen
item.description: A monument built by a fascist that was meant to honor people who died in the Spanish Civil War, but is also a mass grave of the people he murdered

item.title: California Love Scared Straight
item.description: Old taggers meet their anti-tagging arch-nemesis, and how they have turned out after all these years

item.title: Policing the Open Road
item.description: How cars transformed American freedom

item.title: The Revolutionary Post (Repeat)
item.description: How the Post Office created America

item.title: Podcast Episode
item.description: The no-brand that changed the way we eat, shop, and ironically, think about branding

item.title: Where Do We Go From Here?
item.description: Many transgender, non-binary, and intersex people risk stress and sometimes physical danger when entering bathrooms that are segregated by sex. But a group of people have devised a design solution that may make bathrooms better for everyone.

item.title: Highways 101
item.description: Icons and symbols and signage are all around us, and nowhere more so than on the open road.

item.title: The Address Book
item.description: An address is something many people take for granted today, but they are in fact a fairly recent invention that has shaped our cities and taken on great political importance.

item.title: Goodnight Nobody
item.description: The unlikely battle between the creator of the New York Public Library's children's reading room and Goodnight Moon

item.title: Exploring The 99% Invisible City
item.description: Roman and Kurt describe Oakland as it is

item.title: For the Love of Peat
item.description: Protecting and restoring an extremely important but historically maligned ecosystem

item.title: Sign Stealing
item.description: The Houston Astros and the long storied history of sign stealing in baseball

item.title: 99pi Presents The Next Billion Users
item.description: Designing for the Next Billion Users

item.title: Take a Walk
item.description: Taking a walk with our friends at Pop Up Magazine

item.title: The Lost Cities of Geo
item.description: The catastrophic and dramatic fall of a bunch of cities that no longer exist

item.title: You've Got Enron Mail!
item.description: Enron collapsed nearly 20 years ago, but chances are something you use today was affected by emails sent by 150 of the company's top employees.

item.title: In The Unlikely Event
item.description: If you’ve ever flown on a plane, you’ve been directed to study the safety briefing card in your seatback pocket.

item.title: Sean Exploder
item.description: Sean Exploder is a podcast where Sean Real takes apart her songs, and piece by piece, tells the story of how they were made.

item.title: The Great Indoors
item.description: Emily Anthes looks at all of the ways our indoor spaces impact our health, and observes that there is so much we don't really know about the places we spend a majority of our lives.

item.title: According to Need coming December 1
item.description: Trailer for the new 99% Invisible limited series According to Need

item.title: According to Need: Prologue
item.description: Introductory chapter to According to Need

item.title: Chapter 1: Tulicia
item.description: Tulicia reaches out for help

item.title: Chapter 2: The Hotline
item.description: Katie spends a day in the 211 call center

item.title: Chapter 3: Housing First
item.description: Sam Tsemberis radically rethinks how to solve homelessness

item.title: Chapter 4: The List
item.description: The secrets of the list that determines who gets what kind of assistance

item.title: Chapter 5: Housing Finally
item.description: If homelessness is the problem, housing is the solution. But it’s not always that simple.

item.title: Roman Mars on Bullseye with Jesse Thorn
item.description: Roman talks to Jesse Thorn
